Point cloud registration is described as a fundamental and challenging task in 3D computer vision, involving aligning partially overlapping point clouds through estimating their relative rigid transformation.
Point cloud registration aims to align two partially overlapping point clouds by estimating their relative rigid transformation, which includes 3D rotation and 3D translation.
Point cloud registration has potential applications such as Lidar SLAM, which involves aligning two partially overlapping point clouds by estimating their relative rigid transformation.
Point cloud registration is important for tasks such as 3D scene reconstruction, which involves aligning two partially overlapping point clouds by estimating their relative rigid transformation.
Point cloud registration is essential for tasks such as object pose estimation, which involves aligning two partially overlapping point clouds by estimating their relative rigid transformation.
Point cloud registration involves extracting point descriptors and establishing correspondences between two point clouds to obtain the transformation geometrically.
Establishing correspondences between point descriptors is crucial in point cloud registration as it helps in obtaining the transformation between two point clouds.
Efforts have been made to design both traditional and deep learning-based descriptors for point cloud registration.
The traditional and deep learning-based descriptors have been designed to deal with outliers in the resulting correspondences, but they still suffer from outliers, particularly in challenging cases.

Outlier filtering strategies have been developed to robustify the registration process, which includes traditional rejection methods, point-wise descriptor similarity, and group-wise spatial consistency.

Outlier filtering strategies have been developed, including the current state-of-the-art method PointDSC, which relies on a spatial consistency-driven non-local network to capture long-range context in its learned correspondence features.

PointDSC method relies on a spatial consistency-driven non-local network to capture long-range context in its learned correspondence features, but it still yields limited registration robustness, particularly for scenes with a high outlier ratio.

High outlier ratios lead to ambiguities in the learned correspondence features, particularly for scenes with a high outlier ratio, where the spatial consistency constraints may become ambiguous, degrading the correspondence features’ quality.

Ambiguities arising from high outlier ratios can be explicitly accounted for by developing a probabilistic feature learning framework, which aims to address the ambiguities by considering the probabilistic nature of the features.
The variational non-local network is based on an attention mechanism to learn discriminative feature representations for robust outlier rejection within the probabilistic feature learning framework.
A random feature is injected in each query, key, and value component in the non-local network to capture the ambiguous nature of long-range contextual dependencies.
The prior/posterior distributions of random features are predicted by prior/posterior encoders to make the feature distribution label-dependent.
During training, the prior distribution is pushed close to the label-dependent posterior distribution, enabling the prior encoder to learn discriminative features.
A probabilistic graphical model is customized over the variational non-local network to achieve effective variational inference.

The probabilistic graphical model characterizes the conditional dependencies of random features, leading to the derivation of a variational lower bound as the optimization objective for network training.
A voting-based deterministic inlier searching mechanism is proposed for transformation estimation using correspondence features learned from all non-local iterations.
The searching mechanism is used for transformation estimation by learning correspondence features and voting for high-confidence hypothetical inliers.
Correspondence features learned from all non-local iterations jointly vote for high-confidence hypothetical inliers in transformation estimation.

The deterministic inlier searching strategy is analyzed for robustness compared to RANSAC, leading to the design of a conservative seed selection mechanism to improve robustness in sparse point clouds.
A novel variational non-local network is proposed for outlier rejection by learning discriminative correspondence features with Bayesian-driven long-range contextual dependencies.
A probabilistic graphical model is customized over the variational non-local network to derive the variational low bound for effective model optimization.
A Wilson score-based voting mechanism is introduced to search high-quality hypothetical inliers and is theoretically proven to be superior to RANSAC.
The experimental results demonstrate that the framework outperforms the state-of-the-art registration methods on extensive benchmark datasets.

The more inliers in Cseed, the better for the method, implying that selecting all correspondences in C as seeds theoretically seems the best choice.
Performing inlier selection is necessary to accelerate the registration speed, as fewer seeds may not contain any inlier, leading to registration failure.
In sparse point clouds, performing the original inlier selection may result in fewer seeds even containing no inlier, causing registration failure.
The conservative seed selection strategy changes the seed number to effectively avoid selecting too few inliers in sparse cases while maintaining the original selection strategy in dense cases.
Estimating the optimal transformation parameter is based on the Procrustes method using the voted hypothetical inliers from Cseed.
The optimal transformation parameter is estimated to minimize least-squares errors for each hypothetical inlier group.
Selecting the transformation parameter that maximizes the number of overlapped correspondences results in the final optimal transformation estimation.

VBNonlocal is compared to SCNonlocal to determine if it can learn more discriminative features. The distribution of feature similarity of inliers on 3DMatch and 3DLoMatch datasets is visualized to show that VBNonlocal produces features with much higher similarities than SCNonlocal.

Classification loss is added to features produced by each iteration in SCNonlocal to guide their learning. This is denoted as SCNonlocalcls. However, the addition of classification loss only achieves very limited performance gain and sometimes degrades the score, indicating the limitations of loss-based label-propagation for feature learning.

SCNonlocal demonstrates the superiority of label-dependent posterior guidance for feature learning. This guidance leads to significantly faster convergence speed compared to SCNonlocal, as shown in the training curves.
The proposed Bayesian-inspired non-local network promotes more discriminative correspondence-feature learning with much higher similarities compared to SC-Nonlocal on both datasets.

The proposed voting-based inlier searching strategy consistently achieves performance improvement, as seen in the sixth row of Table 3, benefiting from the high-quality hypothetical inliers sampled by the voting policy.

The proposed conservative seed selection strategy achieves consistent performance gain in each setting, as observed in the seventh row of Table 3, with much more significant improvement in cases of fewer points like in the FCGF setting with 250 and 500 points.
The FCGF setting with 250 and 500 points shows a much more significant improvement of +3.4% and +2.5%.
The original inlier selection strategy like in [2] is aggressive and prone to miss too many inlier seeds.
The original inlier selection strategy is aggressive while the conservative selection strategy is effective in mitigating the aggressiveness and maintaining registration efficiency.
The non-local network was enhanced by adapting variational Bayesian inference to develop effective Bayesian-guided long-term dependencies for discriminative correspondence-feature learning.
Variational inference was achieved effectively by customizing a probabilistic graphical model over the non-local network.
The Wilson score-based voting mechanism for inlier sampling was theoretically confirmed to be superior to RANSAC.
Extensive experiments conducted on indoor/outdoor datasets demonstrated the promising performance of the proposed methods.
